docs.text:
Math Kickstarter

Self-paced mathematics brush-up for engineering students at VIA University College

docs.title: About this site
docs.text:
Math Kickstarter is an online refresher course covering foundational mathematics topics. It is designed for students who need to strengthen their skills before — or during — their engineering programme.

This site is not a replacement for the full mathematics curriculum — it covers selected foundational topics through short video lectures, self-check quizzes, and exercises with solutions organised into five topic areas.

docs.title: Before you begin: the pre-test
docs.text:
Have you taken the pre-test?

Before using this site, you should complete the mathematics pre-test provided by your programme.

  • If you score 75% or above, you generally do not need to work through this material.
  • If you score below 75%, you should use this site to review the topics where you need support.

Your pre-test result will indicate which topic areas to focus on. Follow those recommendations rather than working through every topic from start to finish.

Take the pre-test here

docs.title: How to use this site
docs.text:
1. Take the pre-test and note which topic areas you should review.
  2. Open the relevant topic(s) from the Topics menu.
  3. Watch the videos in the order shown on each topic page.
  4. Take the quiz at the bottom of the topic page. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding of the material.
  5. Work through the exercises. Use the collapsible answers to check your work.

Get settled with pen and paper — complete the exercises without a calculator or maths software. You may use a standard mathematical formula sheet and the Tutorials on this site.

You can revisit any topic at any time. Quizzes are for self-assessment only — results are not stored or reported.

If you need several topics, we suggest starting with Basic Arithmetic and Equations and progressing through to Vectors in 2D — see the Topics overview for details.

docs.title: Index
docs.text:
Topics

Click a topic in the menu (or in the table below) to access videos, a self-check quiz, and exercises for that area. Work through the topics recommended to you after the pre-test, or review any topic you want to strengthen.

docs.title: What this course covers
docs.text:
Topic Subtopics Basic Arithmetic and Equations Order of operations, fractions, exponents and radicals, parentheses, equations, quadratic equations, rearranging formulae Functions Functions and graphs, common function types, inverse functions Differential Calculus Derivatives and tangents, differentiation rules Integral Calculus Indefinite integrals and antiderivatives, definite integrals and areas Vectors in 2D Vectors in 2D, dot product, parametric equation of a line

Some advanced techniques are deliberately excluded (for example the product, quotient, and chain rules for differentiation, and integration by substitution or partial fractions). The focus is on the core skills needed for engineering mathematics.

docs.title: Recommended order
docs.text:
If you need to work through several topics, we suggest the following order:

  1. Basic Arithmetic and Equations
  2. Functions
  3. Differential Calculus
  4. Integral Calculus
  5. Vectors in 2D

Within each topic, watch the videos in the order they appear on the page before taking the quiz and working on the exercises.

docs.title: Basic Arithmetic and Equations
docs.text:
Basic Arithmetic and Equations

Work through the videos below in order. When you have watched them, take the quiz and try the exercises. A quiz score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
After working through this topic, you should be able to:

  • Apply the correct order of operations in arithmetic expressions
  • Add, subtract, multiply, and divide fractions
  • Work with exponents and radicals
  • Expand and simplify expressions involving parentheses, including \((a+b)^2\)
  • Solve linear and simple quadratic equations
  • Rearrange formulae to make a different variable the subject

docs.title: 1. Order of operations
docs.text: Apply the order of operations (PEMDAS): parentheses, exponents, multiplication and division, then addition and subtraction.

docs.title: 2. Fractions
docs.text: Work with numerators and denominators, find common denominators, and add, multiply, and divide fractions.

docs.title: 3. Exponents and radicals
docs.text: Use the laws of exponents, including negative and zero powers, and simplify expressions with square roots.

docs.title: 4. Parentheses
docs.text: Expand brackets using the distributive law and recognise special products such as \((a+b)^2\).

docs.title: 5. Equations
docs.text: Understand what the equals sign means and solve linear equations by applying the same operation to both sides.

docs.title: 6. Quadratic equations
docs.text: Write quadratic equations in standard form, use the quadratic formula, and interpret the discriminant.

docs.title: 7. Rearranging formulae
docs.text: Make a different variable the subject of a formula by undoing operations in reverse order.

docs.title: Quiz
docs.text:
When you have been through the videos, test your understanding with the quiz below. The quiz contains 10 questions. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding. You can see your total score at the bottom of the page, as well as your score for each question. You can retake the quiz as many times as you like — results are not stored or assessed.

Question 1In the expression $\dfrac{3 + 2 \times 6}{5}$, which statement best describes how it should be evaluated?Add 3 and 2 first because addition comes before divisionTreat the numerator as having an implicit grouping: compute $3 + 2 \times 6$ before dividing by 5Divide 2 by 5 before multiplying by 6Evaluate the denominator before the numeratorCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: A fraction bar groups the numerator as a single expression. Inside the numerator, multiplication comes before addition, so $3 + 12 = 15$, and then $15 \div 5 = 3$.

Question 2What is the value of $6 - 2 \times 5^2$?$200$$-44$$16$$-16$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Exponentiation comes first: $5^2 = 25$. Then multiplication: $2 \times 25 = 50$. Finally subtraction: $6 - 50 = -44$.

Question 3Which step is **essential** when adding $\dfrac{5}{6}$ and $\dfrac{1}{4}$?Multiply the numerators directlyFind a common denominator before adding the numeratorsAdd the denominators and keep the larger numeratorConvert both fractions to decimals and roundCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Fractions can only be added directly when they share a denominator. Here the least common multiple of 6 and 4 is 12, giving $\dfrac{10}{12} + \dfrac{3}{12} = \dfrac{13}{12}$.

Question 4Which simplification is correct?$\dfrac{a^5}{a^2} = a^{10}$$\dfrac{a^5}{a^2} = a^3$$\dfrac{a^5}{a^2} = a^{2.5}$$\dfrac{a^5}{a^2} = 3a$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: When dividing powers with the same base, subtract the exponents: $a^{5-2} = a^3$.

Question 5Which expression is equal to $2^{-3}$?$-8$$\dfrac{1}{8}$$-6$$\dfrac{1}{6}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: A negative exponent means reciprocal: $2^{-3} = \dfrac{1}{2^3} = \dfrac{1}{8}$. It does not mean the base becomes negative.

Question 6Which expansion is correct?$2r - (3 - r) = 2r - 3 - r$$2r - (3 - r) = 3r - 3$$2r - (3 - r) = r - 3$$2r - (3 - r) = 2r - 3 + r - 3$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Subtracting a bracket means changing the sign of every term inside: $2r - 3 + r = 3r - 3$.

Question 7The equation $4x - 2 = 18 - x$ is solved by first adding $x$ to both sides. Why is this a valid first step?Because $x$ must always be moved to the left-hand sideBecause it collects all $x$-terms on one side while keeping the equation balancedBecause subtraction is always done before additionBecause the right-hand side is larger than the left-hand sideCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The goal is to isolate $x$. Adding $x$ to both sides gives $5x - 2 = 18$, which gathers the variable terms without changing the solution set.

Question 8Before applying the quadratic formula to $4x(x + 1) + 3 = 2$, what must you do first?Divide every term by 4Expand and simplify to write the equation in standard form $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$Take the square root of both sides immediatelySet $x = 0$ and stopCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The quadratic formula applies only after the equation is in standard form. Expanding gives $4x^2 + 4x + 1 = 0$.

Question 9For $2x^2 - 4x - 6 = 0$, what does the discriminant tell you?There are no real solutions because $b$ is negativeThere are two distinct real solutions because the discriminant is positiveThere is exactly one solution because the leading coefficient is 2The solutions must be fractions because $c$ is negativeCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Here $b^2 - 4ac = (-4)^2 - 4(2)(-6) = 16 + 48 = 64 > 0$, so there are two distinct real solutions.

Question 10To make $r$ the subject of $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$, which sequence of inverse operations is correct?Divide by $\pi$, then subtract $\dfrac{4}{3}$, then take the square rootDivide by $\dfrac{4}{3}\pi$, then take the cube rootMultiply by 3, then divide by 4, then squareTake the cube root first, then divide by $V$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Undo the operations in reverse order: first divide both sides by $\dfrac{4}{3}\pi$ to isolate $r^3$, then take the cube root to get $r = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3V}{4\pi}}$.

docs.title: Exercise 5
docs.text:
Solve for the variable:

  1. \(7x + 4 = 22 - x\)
  2. \(\dfrac{3y - 1}{4} = y + 2\)
  1. \(8x = 18 \Rightarrow x = \dfrac{9}{4}\)
  2. \(3y - 1 = 4y + 8 \Rightarrow -y = 9 \Rightarrow y = -9\)

docs.title: Exercise 7
docs.text:
For each equation, find the discriminant and state the number of real solutions:

  1. \(2x^2 + x - 3 = 0\)
  2. \(x^2 + 4x + 7 = 0\)
  1. \(\Delta = 1 + 24 = 25 > 0\), so two distinct real solutions
  2. \(\Delta = 16 - 28 = -12 < 0\), so no real solutions

docs.title: Exercise 8
docs.text:
Rearrange each formula to make the requested variable the subject:

  1. \(E = mc^2\) for \(c\)
  2. \(T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g}}\) for \(L\)
  1. \(c^2 = \dfrac{E}{m}\), so \(c = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{m}}\) (taking the non-negative root)
  2. \(\dfrac{T^2}{4\pi^2} = \dfrac{L}{g}\), so \(L = \dfrac{gT^2}{4\pi^2}\)

docs.title: Exercise 9
docs.text:
Evaluate:

\[\left(\dfrac{3}{4} - \dfrac{1}{8}\right)^{2} + \sqrt{\dfrac{9}{16}}\]

\(\left(\dfrac{5}{8}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{3}{4} = \dfrac{25}{64} + \dfrac{48}{64} = \dfrac{73}{64}\)

docs.title: Exercise 10
docs.text:
Solve for \(x\):

\[\dfrac{2(x + 3)}{5} + \dfrac{x - 1}{2} = 4\]

Show your working and verify the solution.

Multiply through by 10: \(4(x + 3) + 5(x - 1) = 40\)

\(4x + 12 + 5x - 5 = 40 \Rightarrow 9x = 33 \Rightarrow x = \dfrac{11}{3}\)

Check: \(\dfrac{8}{3} + \dfrac{4}{3} = 4\) ✓

docs.title: Differential Calculus
docs.text:
Differential Calculus

Work through the videos below in order. When you have watched them, take the quiz and try the exercises. A quiz score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
After working through this topic, you should be able to:

  • Interpret the derivative as the slope of a tangent line
  • Differentiate simple power functions and common expressions
  • Apply basic differentiation rules (this topic does not cover product, quotient, or chain rule)

docs.title: 1. Derivatives and tangents
docs.text: Interpret the derivative as the slope of a tangent and as a rate of change.

docs.title: 2. Differentiation rules
docs.text: Differentiate sums, differences, and constant multiples, and use common derivative formulas.

docs.title: Quiz
docs.text:
When you have been through the videos, test your understanding with the quiz below. The quiz contains 10 questions. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding. You can see your total score at the bottom of the page, as well as your score for each question. You can retake the quiz as many times as you like — results are not stored or assessed.

Question 1What is the derivative of $f(x) = x^3$?$3x$$3x^2$$x^2$$3x^3$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Using the power rule, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^n) = nx^{n-1}$, so $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^3) = 3x^2$.

Question 2Geometrically, what does $f'(a)$ represent at a point on the graph of $f$?The $y$-coordinate of the point $(a, f(a))$The slope of the tangent line to the graph at $x = a$The area under the graph from $0$ to $a$The average slope of the graph over the interval $[0, a]$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The derivative at a point gives the instantaneous rate of change of $f$, which equals the slope of the tangent line at that point.

Question 3If $f'(x) > 0$ for all $x$ in an interval, what can we conclude about $f$ on that interval?$f$ is decreasing$f$ is increasing$f$ has a local maximum$f$ is constantCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: A positive derivative means the function's output is rising as $x$ increases, so $f$ is increasing on that interval.

Question 4At a local maximum of a differentiable function $f$, what must be true about $f'(x)$ at that point?$f'(x) = 1$$f'(x) = 0$$f'(x)$ is undefined$f'(x) < 0$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: At a local maximum the tangent line is horizontal, so the slope — and hence the derivative — is zero.

Question 5What is $\dfrac{d}{dx}(e^x)$?$xe^{x-1}$$e^x$$\dfrac{1}{x}$$e$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The exponential function $e^x$ is its own derivative: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(e^x) = e^x$.

Question 6What is $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\ln x)$?$e^x$$\dfrac{1}{x}$$x$$\ln x$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The derivative of the natural logarithm is $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\ln x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ (for $x > 0$).

Question 7What is $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x)$?$-\sin x$$\cos x$$-\cos x$$\sin x$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Differentiating sine gives cosine: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = \cos x$.

Question 8What is $\dfrac{d}{dx}(4x^2 - 3x + 7)$?$4x - 3$$8x - 3$$8x^2 - 3$$4x^2 - 3$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Apply the sum/difference and constant-multiple rules together with the power rule: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(4x^2) = 8x$, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(-3x) = -3$, and the derivative of the constant $7$ is $0$.

Question 9Which of the following is **not** covered in this topic?The constant multiple ruleThe chain ruleThe power rule for $x^n$The derivative of $\cos x$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: This topic covers basic rules and common derivatives only. The product rule, quotient rule, and chain rule are left for later study.

Question 10Using the constant multiple rule, what is $\dfrac{d}{dx}(5\sin x)$?$5\cos x + C$$5\cos x$$\sin(5x)$$5\sin x$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The constant multiple rule gives $\dfrac{d}{dx}(5\sin x) = 5 \cdot \dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = 5\cos x$. Note that $+C$ belongs to integration, not differentiation.

docs.title: Exercise 1
docs.text:
Differentiate the following:

  1. \(f(x) = 4x^5\)
  2. \(f(x) = 2x^3 - 5x + 4\)
  1. \(f'(x) = 20x^4\)
  2. \(f'(x) = 6x^2 - 5\)

docs.title: Exercise 3
docs.text:
Differentiate \(f(x) = 7e^x - 3\).

\(f'(x) = 7e^x\)

docs.title: Exercise 4
docs.text:
Differentiate \(f(x) = 5\ln x\).

\(f'(x) = \dfrac{5}{x}\)

docs.title: Exercise 5
docs.text:
Differentiate \(f(x) = 3\cos x - \sin x\).

\(f'(x) = -3\sin x - \cos x\)

docs.title: Exercise 6
docs.text:
Differentiate \(f(x) = 4\cos x + 2\sin x + 3e^x\).

\(f'(x) = -4\sin x + 2\cos x + 3e^x\)

docs.title: Exercise 7
docs.text:
Find the value(s) of \(x\) where \(f'(x) = 0\) for \(f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 5\).

\(f'(x) = 2x - 6\), so \(2x - 6 = 0\) gives \(x = 3\).

docs.title: Exercise 8
docs.text:
For \(f(x) = x^2 + 4x - 5\), find \(f'(x)\) and state whether \(f\) is increasing or decreasing at \(x = 2\).

\(f'(x) = 2x + 4\). At \(x = 2\), \(f'(2) = 8 > 0\), so \(f\) is increasing at \(x = 2\).

docs.title: Exercise 10
docs.text:
The graph of \(f(x) = x^3 - 12x\) has a local maximum and a local minimum. Find where \(f'(x) = 0\) and state which value of \(x\) gives the local maximum.

\(f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12 = 3(x^2 - 4) = 3(x - 2)(x + 2)\), so \(f'(x) = 0\) at \(x = -2\) and \(x = 2\).

\(f(-2) = -8 + 24 = 16\) and \(f(2) = 8 - 24 = -16\), so the local maximum occurs at \(x = -2\).

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
Functions

Work through the videos below in order. When you have watched them, take the quiz and try the exercises. A quiz score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
After working through this topic, you should be able to:

  • Explain domain, range, and the graph of a function
  • Recognise and work with linear, power, and exponential functions
  • Understand and find inverse functions where they exist

docs.title: 1. Functions and graphs
docs.text: See functions as input–output rules, identify domain and range, and read values from graphs.

docs.title: 2. Common function types
docs.text: Recognise linear, power, and exponential functions and how each one grows or changes.

docs.title: 3. Inverse functions
docs.text: Find inverse functions where they exist and connect exponentials with logarithms.

docs.title: Quiz
docs.text:
When you have been through the videos, test your understanding with the quiz below. The quiz contains 10 questions. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding. You can see your total score at the bottom of the page, as well as your score for each question. You can retake the quiz as many times as you like — results are not stored or assessed.

Question 1Which of the following best describes the domain of a function?The set of all output values of the functionThe set of all input values for which the function is definedThe set of points where the graph crosses the $x$-axisThe highest value the function can takeCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The domain is the set of all values that can be used as input to the function.

Question 2What is the range of $f(x) = x^2$?All real numbersAll real numbers greater than or equal to $0$All real numbers except $0$All integersCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Squaring any real number gives a result $\ge 0$, and every non-negative value is achieved (e.g. $f(0)=0$, $f(2)=4$). So the range is $[0, \infty)$.

Question 3If $f(x) = 3x - 4$, what is $f(5)$?$7$$11$$15$$-1$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Substitute $x = 5$: $f(5) = 3(5) - 4 = 15 - 4 = 11$.

Question 4Which of the following is a linear function?$f(x) = x^2 + 1$$f(x) = -2x + 7$$f(x) = 2^x$$f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: A linear function has the form $f(x) = ax + b$, where $a$ and $b$ are constants. Only $-2x + 7$ fits this form.

Question 5The function $f(x) = 5x^3$ is an example of which type?An exponential functionA power functionA linear functionA logarithmic functionCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: A power function has the form $f(x) = ax^n$, where the variable appears as a base raised to a fixed exponent. Here $f(x) = 5x^3$.

Question 6For $f(x) = 4 \cdot 3^x$, what are the initial value and the base?Initial value $3$, base $4$Initial value $4$, base $3$Initial value $4$, base $x$Initial value $1$, base $12$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: In $f(x) = b \cdot a^x$, $b$ is the initial value (when $x = 0$, $f(0) = b$) and $a$ is the base. So $b = 4$ and $a = 3$.

Question 7Under what condition does a function have an inverse function?It must be a linear functionIt must be one-to-one (each output comes from exactly one input)It must pass through the originIts domain must be all real numbersCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: An inverse exists only when the function is one-to-one. If two different inputs give the same output, you cannot uniquely reverse the mapping.

Question 8To find the inverse of $y = f(x)$ algebraically, which step comes first?Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$Swap $x$ and $y$ in the equationSquare both sidesReplace $x$ with $f^{-1}(x)$ immediatelyCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Finding an inverse means reversing input and output. Swapping $x$ and $y$ reflects this reversal before solving for the new output variable.

Question 9The graph of $f^{-1}$ is the reflection of the graph of $f$ across which line?The $x$-axisThe line $y = x$The $y$-axisThe line $y = -x$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Points $(a, b)$ on $f$ correspond to $(b, a)$ on $f^{-1}$. Swapping coordinates reflects the graph across the line $y = x$.

Question 10Which statement correctly links logarithms and exponentials?$\log_a(x)$ is the same as $a^x$$\log_a(x)$ is the inverse of $a^x$$\log_a(x) = x^a$$\log_a(x)$ only exists when $a = e$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: If $y = a^x$, then $x = \log_a(y)$. The logarithm undoes exponentiation, so $\log_a$ and $a^x$ are inverse functions (for $a > 0$, $a \ne 1$).

docs.title: Exercise 1
docs.text:
Find the domain and range of \(f(x) = \sqrt{x + 4}\).

Domain: \(x + 4 \ge 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x \ge -4\), i.e. \([-4, \infty)\).

Range: square roots are non-negative, so \([0, \infty)\).

docs.title: Exercise 3
docs.text:
Identify each function as linear, power, or exponential:

  1. \(f(x) = 4x + 1\)
  2. \(g(x) = 5x^{-2}\)
  3. \(p(x) = 3 \cdot 2^x\)
  1. Linear
  2. Power (\(g(x) = 5x^{-2}\))
  3. Exponential

docs.title: Exercise 4
docs.text:
Rearrange each formula to make the indicated variable the subject:

  1. \(d = vt\) — solve for \(t\)
  2. \(F = kx\) — solve for \(x\)
  3. \(V = \pi r^2 h\) — solve for \(h\)
  1. \(t = \dfrac{d}{v}\)
  2. \(x = \dfrac{F}{k}\)
  3. \(h = \dfrac{V}{\pi r^2}\)

docs.title: Exercise 6
docs.text:
Find the inverse of \(f(x) = 5x + 1\). State the domain and range of both \(f\) and \(f^{-1}\).

\(y = 5x + 1 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 5y + 1 \;\Rightarrow\; y = \dfrac{x - 1}{5}\)

So \(f^{-1}(x) = \dfrac{x - 1}{5}\).

Domain of \(f\): all real numbers; range of \(f\): all real numbers.

Domain of \(f^{-1}\): all real numbers; range of \(f^{-1}\): all real numbers.

docs.title: Exercise 7
docs.text:
Ohm's law is \(V = IR\). Rearrange to express \(I\) in terms of \(V\) and \(R\), then find \(I\) when \(V = 12\,\text{V}\) and \(R = 4\,\Omega\).

\(I = \dfrac{V}{R}\)

\(I = \dfrac{12}{4} = 3\,\text{A}\)

docs.title: Exercise 8
docs.text:
For \(f(x) = 2^x\) and \(g(x) = \log_2(x)\):

  1. Evaluate \(f(4)\)
  2. Evaluate \(g(32)\)
  3. Simplify \(g(f(x))\)
  1. \(f(4) = 2^4 = 16\)
  2. \(g(32) = \log_2(32) = 5\) because \(2^5 = 32\)
  3. \(g(f(x)) = \log_2(2^x) = x\) (for \(x > 0\) when using real logarithms)

docs.title: Exercise 9
docs.text:
For \(f(x) = 4x + 1\), find \(f^{-1}(x)\) and state the line of symmetry shared by the graphs of \(f\) and \(f^{-1}\).

\(f^{-1}(x) = \dfrac{x - 1}{4}\)

The graphs are reflections of each other across the line \(y = x\).

docs.title: Exercise 10
docs.text:
A bacteria culture grows according to \(N(t) = 200 \cdot 3^t\), where \(t\) is time in hours.

  1. What is the initial population?
  2. What is the population after 2 hours?
  3. Rearrange the formula to express \(t\) in terms of \(N\) (use logarithms).
  1. \(N(0) = 200 \cdot 3^0 = 200\)
  2. \(N(2) = 200 \cdot 3^2 = 200 \cdot 9 = 1800\)
  3. \(\dfrac{N}{200} = 3^t\), so \(t = \log_3\!\left(\dfrac{N}{200}\right)\)

docs.title: Integral Calculus
docs.text:
Integral Calculus

Work through the videos below in order. When you have watched them, take the quiz and try the exercises. A quiz score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
After working through this topic, you should be able to:

  • Find indefinite integrals (antiderivatives) of simple functions
  • Evaluate definite integrals
  • Interpret definite integrals as area under a curve
  • Apply basic integration techniques only (no substitution or partial fractions)

docs.title: 1. Indefinite integrals and antiderivatives
docs.text: Find antiderivatives and evaluate basic indefinite integrals, including the constant of integration.

docs.title: 2. Definite integrals and areas
docs.text: Evaluate definite integrals and interpret them as signed area under a curve.

docs.title: Quiz
docs.text:
When you have been through the videos, test your understanding with the quiz below. The quiz contains 10 questions. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding. You can see your total score at the bottom of the page, as well as your score for each question. You can retake the quiz as many times as you like — results are not stored or assessed.

Question 1What is $\int 2x \, dx$?$2x^2 + C$$x^2 + C$$x^2$$2 + C$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: An antiderivative of $2x$ is $x^2$, so $\int 2x \, dx = x^2 + C$.

Question 2If $F'(x) = f(x)$, what do we call $F$?A derivative of $f$An antiderivative of $f$The slope of $f$The definite integral of $f$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: An antiderivative (or indefinite integral) of $f$ is a function $F$ whose derivative equals $f$.

Question 3Why do we write $+C$ after an indefinite integral?Because every integral equals zeroBecause antiderivatives are only unique up to an additive constantBecause $C$ is always the value at $x = 0$Because the integral sign requires it for notation onlyCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: If $F'(x) = f(x)$, then so does $(F + C)'(x)$ for any constant $C$. The $+C$ captures every possible antiderivative.

Question 4What is $\int x^4 \, dx$?$4x^3 + C$$\dfrac{x^5}{5} + C$$5x^4 + C$$\dfrac{x^4}{4} + C$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Reverse the power rule: $\int x^n \, dx = \dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C$ for $n \neq -1$, so $\int x^4 \, dx = \dfrac{x^5}{5} + C$.

Question 5What is $\int \dfrac{1}{x} \, dx$?$x^{-2} + C$$\ln|x| + C$$e^x + C$$-\dfrac{1}{x^2} + C$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The antiderivative of $\dfrac{1}{x}$ is the natural logarithm: $\int \dfrac{1}{x} \, dx = \ln|x| + C$ (for $x \neq 0$).

Question 6What is $\int \cos x \, dx$?$-\sin x + C$$\sin x + C$$\cos x + C$$-\cos x + C$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Since $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = \cos x$, we have $\int \cos x \, dx = \sin x + C$.

Question 7If $F'(x) = f(x)$, how is the definite integral $\displaystyle\int_a^b f(x)\, dx$ evaluated?$F(a) - F(b)$$F(b) - F(a)$$f(b) - f(a)$$F(b) + F(a)$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus gives $\displaystyle\int_a^b f(x)\, dx = F(b) - F(a)$, where $F$ is any antiderivative of $f$.

Question 8What is $\displaystyle\int_0^3 2x \, dx$?$6$$9$$3$$18$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: An antiderivative of $2x$ is $x^2$, so $\displaystyle\int_0^3 2x \, dx = [x^2]_0^3 = 9 - 0 = 9$.

Question 9When a function is below the $x$-axis on an interval, how does the definite integral relate to the geometric area?The integral equals the area exactlyThe integral is negative, giving a signed areaThe integral is always zeroThe integral cannot be computedCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Definite integrals measure signed area: regions above the axis contribute positively and regions below contribute negatively.

Question 10What is $\int (3x^2 - 4x + 1) \, dx$?$6x - 4 + C$$x^3 - 2x^2 + x + C$$x^3 - 2x^2 + C$$3x^3 - 4x^2 + x + C$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Integrate term by term: $\int 3x^2 \, dx = x^3$, $\int -4x \, dx = -2x^2$, and $\int 1 \, dx = x$, giving $x^3 - 2x^2 + x + C$.

docs.title: Exercise 1
docs.text:
Evaluate:

  1. \(\int (5x^2 + 1) \, dx\)
  2. \(\displaystyle\int_0^3 3x \, dx\)
  1. \(\dfrac{5x^3}{3} + x + C\)
  2. \(\left[\dfrac{3x^2}{2}\right]_0^3 = \dfrac{27}{2}\)

docs.title: Exercise 3
docs.text:
Evaluate \(\int \dfrac{4}{x} \, dx\).

\(4\ln|x| + C\)

docs.title: Exercise 4
docs.text:
Evaluate \(\int (2\cos x + 4\sin x) \, dx\).

\(2\sin x - 4\cos x + C\)

docs.title: Exercise 5
docs.text:
Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_2^5 (3x + 2) \, dx\).

An antiderivative is \(\dfrac{3x^2}{2} + 2x\), so \(\left[\dfrac{3x^2}{2} + 2x\right]_2^5 = \left(\dfrac{75}{2} + 10\right) - (6 + 4) = \dfrac{75}{2}\).

docs.title: Exercise 6
docs.text:
Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2} \cos x \, dx\).

\([\sin x]_0^{\pi/2} = 1 - 0 = 1\).

docs.title: Exercise 7
docs.text:
Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_{-2}^2 2x \, dx\) and explain the result in terms of signed area.

\(\left[x^2\right]_{-2}^2 = 4 - 4 = 0\). The graph of \(y = 2x\) lies below the axis on \([-2, 0]\) and above on \([0, 2]\); equal signed areas cancel.

docs.title: Exercise 8
docs.text:
Find the area between the graph of \(f(x) = x^2\) and the \(x\)-axis from \(x = 0\) to \(x = 2\).

\(\displaystyle\int_0^2 x^2 \, dx = \left[\dfrac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^2 = \dfrac{8}{3}\). Since \(x^2 \geq 0\) on \([0, 2]\), the integral equals the geometric area.

docs.title: Exercise 9
docs.text:
Verify that \(F(x) = \dfrac{x^3}{3} + 4x\) is an antiderivative of \(f(x) = x^2 + 4\) by differentiating \(F\), then evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_0^1 (x^2 + 4) \, dx\).

\(F'(x) = x^2 + 4 = f(x)\), so \(F\) is an antiderivative. \(\displaystyle\int_0^1 (x^2 + 4) \, dx = F(1) - F(0) = \left(\dfrac{1}{3} + 4\right) - 0 = \dfrac{13}{3}\).

docs.title: Exercise 10
docs.text:
A velocity function is \(v(t) = t^2 + 1\) (in m/s). Find the displacement from \(t = 0\) to \(t = 3\) seconds.

Displacement \(= \displaystyle\int_0^3 (t^2 + 1) \, dt = \left[\dfrac{t^3}{3} + t\right]_0^3 = (9 + 3) - 0 = 12\) m.

docs.title: FAQ
docs.text: FAQ

docs.title: General information
docs.text:
What is Math Kickstarter?

Math Kickstarter is a self-paced online brush-up course covering foundational mathematics topics for engineering students at VIA University College. It includes short videos, self-check quizzes, and exercises with solutions.

Who should use this site?

This site is intended for students who scored below 75% on the mathematics pre-test. If you scored 75% or above, you generally do not need to work through this material.

Do I need to complete every topic?

No. Use the pre-test results to identify which topic areas you should review. Focus on those topics rather than working through the entire site from start to finish.

What topics are covered?

The site covers five areas: Basic Arithmetic and Equations, Functions, Differential Calculus, Integral Calculus, and Vectors in 2D. See the Topics overview for details.

Are advanced calculus techniques included?

No. This is a brush-up course. Topics such as the product, quotient, and chain rules for differentiation, and integration by substitution or partial fractions, are deliberately excluded.

docs.title: Using the site
docs.text:
In what order should I watch the videos?

Within each topic, watch the videos in the order they appear on the page. If you need several topics, we recommend starting with Basic Arithmetic and Equations and progressing through to Vectors in 2D.

What does a quiz score of 7/10 mean?

The quizzes are for self-assessment only. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests you have sufficient understanding of the material in that topic. Results are not stored or reported.

Where are the exercise solutions?

Solutions are available on each topic page. Click the answer blocks to reveal the solution.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text:
Who should I contact if I have questions about the material?

Contact Assistant Professor Mette Bjerg, memo@via.dk, for questions about the course content.

Who should I contact about the pre-test?

Contact Associate Professor Richard Brooks, rib@via.dk, for questions about the pre-test.

Who should I contact about my specific programme requirements?

Contact your programme coordinator for questions about your specific programme requirements.

docs.title: Index
docs.text:
Tutorials

Step-by-step tutorials organised by topic. Select a tutorial from the Tutorials menu or the table below.

Each page matches one of the five main course areas on the site. Subsections correspond to specific skills or video themes. Return to the topic pages for videos, quizzes, and exercises.

Complete exercises with pen and paper — no calculator or maths software — but you may use a standard formula sheet and these tutorials.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text: Topic Subsections Basic Arithmetic and Equations Order of operations, fractions, exponents and radicals, parentheses, equations, quadratic equations, rearranging formulae Functions Functions and graphs, common function types, inverse functions Differential Calculus Derivatives and tangents, differentiation rules Integral Calculus Indefinite integrals and antiderivatives, definite integrals and areas Vectors in 2D Vectors in 2D, dot product, parametric equation of a line (optional)

docs.title: How to use the tutorials
docs.text:
1. Open the tutorial page for the topic you are studying.
  2. Work through the subsections in order, using pen and paper for the examples.
  3. Check your work against the topic page exercises when you are ready.

Tutorial content is being added topic by topic. Pages marked with placeholders are still in preparation.

docs.title: Basic Arithmetic and Equations
docs.text:
Basic Arithmetic and Equations

Work through these tutorials after watching the Basic Arithmetic and Equations videos. Each subsection walks through the main ideas step by step with worked examples. The exercises on the topic page use different problems — they are not copies of the tutorial or video examples.

docs.title: 1. Order of operations
docs.text:
When an expression contains several operations, use a fixed priority order (often remembered as PEMDAS):

  1. Parentheses ( ) — work inside brackets first
  2. Exponentiation — powers such as \(5^2\)
  3. Multiplication and division — from left to right
  4. Addition and subtraction — from left to right

A fraction bar groups the numerator and denominator as single expressions. Evaluate the numerator and denominator separately before dividing.

Example 1: Evaluate \(6 - 2 \times 5^2\).

  1. Exponent: \(5^2 = 25\)
  2. Multiplication: \(2 \times 25 = 50\)
  3. Subtraction: \(6 - 50 = -44\)

Example 2: Evaluate \(\dfrac{3 + 2 \times 6}{5}\).

  1. Inside the numerator, multiply first: \(2 \times 6 = 12\)
  2. Finish the numerator: \(3 + 12 = 15\)
  3. Divide: \(\dfrac{15}{5} = 3\)

Example 3: Evaluate \((6 - 2 \times 5)^2\).

  1. Inside the parentheses: \(6 - 10 = -4\)
  2. Square: \((-4)^2 = 16\)

Example 4: Evaluate \(24 \div 4 \times 3\).

Multiplication and division have equal priority, so work left to right: \(24 \div 4 = 6\), then \(6 \times 3 = 18\).

Check your understanding

Try Exercise 1 on the topic page.

docs.title: 2. Fractions
docs.text:
A fraction \(\dfrac{a}{b}\) has numerator \(a\) (top) and denominator \(b\) (bottom). The denominator tells you into how many equal parts a whole is divided.

Expand or reduce by multiplying or dividing numerator and denominator by the same non-zero number:

\[\dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{k \cdot a}{k \cdot b}\]

Adding or subtracting — denominators must match. If they do not, find a common denominator (often the least common multiple):

\[\dfrac{a}{b} + \dfrac{c}{d} = \dfrac{ad + bc}{bd}\]

Multiplying — multiply numerators and multiply denominators:

\[\dfrac{a}{b} \cdot \dfrac{c}{d} = \dfrac{ac}{bd}\]

Dividing — multiply by the reciprocal of the second fraction:

\[\dfrac{a}{b} \div \dfrac{c}{d} = \dfrac{a}{b} \cdot \dfrac{d}{c} = \dfrac{ad}{bc}\]

Worked example: Simplify \(\dfrac{5}{6} - \dfrac{1}{4}\).

  1. Common denominator of 6 and 4 is 12.
  2. \(\dfrac{5}{6} = \dfrac{10}{12}\) and \(\dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{3}{12}\)
  3. \(\dfrac{10}{12} - \dfrac{3}{12} = \dfrac{7}{12}\)

Worked example: Simplify \(\dfrac{2}{3} \div \dfrac{5}{9}\).

\[\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{9}{5} = \dfrac{18}{15} = \dfrac{6}{5}\] Check your understanding

Try Exercises 2 and 9 on the topic page.

docs.title: 3. Exponents and radicals
docs.text:
Powers: \(a^n\) means \(a\) multiplied by itself \(n\) times (\(n > 0\)).

Laws of exponents (same base \(a \neq 0\) where needed):

Rule Formula Product \(a^n \cdot a^m = a^{n+m}\) Quotient \(\dfrac{a^n}{a^m} = a^{n-m}\) Power of a power \((a^n)^m = a^{nm}\) Power of a product \((a \cdot b)^n = a^n b^n\) Power of a quotient \(\left(\dfrac{a}{b}\right)^n = \dfrac{a^n}{b^n}\) Negative exponent \(a^{-n} = \dfrac{1}{a^n}\) Zero exponent \(a^0 = 1\) Roots and rational exponents \(\sqrt[m]{a^n} = a^{n/m}\)

In particular, \(\sqrt[n]{a} = a^{1/n}\) and \(\sqrt[n]{a^m} = a^{m/n}\) (for \(a \geq 0\) when \(n\) is even, and where the expressions are defined).

Square roots: if \(y^2 = x\), then \(y = \pm\sqrt{x}\). The symbol \(\sqrt{x}\) usually denotes the non-negative square root.

Worked example: Simplify \(\dfrac{2^4 \times 2^{-2}}{2^3}\).

  1. Numerator: \(2^{4+(-2)} = 2^2\)
  2. Quotient: \(\dfrac{2^2}{2^3} = 2^{2-3} = 2^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\)

Worked example: Simplify \(\dfrac{x^3 \cdot x^{-5}}{x^{-2}}\).

\[\dfrac{x^{-2}}{x^{-2}} = x^0 = 1\]

Worked example: Evaluate \(27^{\frac{1}{3}}\) using the root rule.

Using \(a^{n/m} = \sqrt[m]{a^n}\) with \(n = 1\) and \(m = 3\):

\[27^{\frac{1}{3}} = \sqrt[3]{27} = 3\]

Worked example: Write \(\sqrt[4]{16^3}\) as a single power.

\[\sqrt[4]{16^3} = 16^{3/4}\] Check your understanding

Try Exercise 3 on the topic page.

docs.title: 4. Parentheses
docs.text:
Distributive law — multiply each term inside the bracket:

\[a(b + c) = ab + ac\]

Adding a bracket — the signs stay the same:

\[2r + (3 - r) = 2r + 3 - r = r + 3\]

Subtracting a bracket — change the sign of every term inside:

\[2r - (3 - r) = 2r - 3 + r = 3r - 3\]

Special products (learn these patterns):

Pattern Expansion \((a + b)^2\) \(a^2 + 2ab + b^2\) \((a - b)^2\) \(a^2 - 2ab + b^2\) \((a + b)(a - b)\) \(a^2 - b^2\)

Worked example: Expand \(3x + 2(x - 7)\).

\[3x + 2x - 14 = 5x - 14\]

Worked example: Expand \((2 + 3x)^2\).

\[(2 + 3x)^2 = 4 + 12x + 9x^2 = 9x^2 + 12x + 4\] Check your understanding

Try Exercise 4 on the topic page.

docs.title: 5. Equations
docs.text:
The equals sign means the left-hand side and the right-hand side have the same value. It is not a signal to “write the answer next” — it states a balance.

Rules for solving:

  • Perform the same operation on both sides of the equation
  • You may simplify either side, as long as its value does not change
  • Aim to collect variable terms on one side and numbers on the other

Worked example: Solve \(4x - 2 = 18 - x\).

  1. Add \(x\) to both sides: \(5x - 2 = 18\)
  2. Add 2 to both sides: \(5x = 20\)
  3. Divide by 5: \(x = 4\)

Worked example: Solve \(\dfrac{2z + 3}{2} = z + 4\).

  1. Multiply both sides by 2: \(2z + 3 = 2z + 8\)
  2. Subtract \(2z\) from both sides: \(3 = 8\)

This is never true, so there is no solution.

Worked example: Solve \(\dfrac{3(x - 2)}{4} - \dfrac{2(x + 1)}{3} = 1\).

  1. Multiply through by 12 (LCM of 4 and 3): \(9(x - 2) - 8(x + 1) = 12\)
  2. Expand: \(9x - 18 - 8x - 8 = 12\)
  3. Simplify: \(x - 26 = 12\), so \(x = 38\)

Substituting back confirms the solution.

Check your understanding

Try Exercises 5 and 10 on the topic page.

docs.title: 6. Quadratic equations
docs.text:
A quadratic equation in standard form is

\[ax^2 + bx + c = 0 \quad (a \neq 0)\]

Before using the quadratic formula, expand and simplify until the equation is in this form. In this course, use the quadratic formula as the standard method once the equation is in standard form (factoring is not required here).

Quadratic formula:

\[x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}\]

The discriminant is \(\Delta = b^2 - 4ac\):

Discriminant Real solutions \(\Delta > 0\) Two distinct real solutions \(\Delta = 0\) One repeated real solution \(\Delta < 0\) No real solutions

Worked example: Solve \(2x^2 - 4x - 6 = 0\) using the quadratic formula.

  1. Identify coefficients: \(a = 2\), \(b = -4\), \(c = -6\)
  2. Discriminant: \(\Delta = (-4)^2 - 4(2)(-6) = 16 + 48 = 64\)
  3. Apply the formula: \(x = \frac{-(-4) \pm \sqrt{64}}{2(2)} = \frac{4 \pm 8}{4}\)
  4. Two solutions: \(x = \dfrac{12}{4} = 3\) or \(x = \dfrac{-4}{4} = -1\)

Worked example: Solve \(4x(x + 1) + 3 = 2\) using the quadratic formula.

  1. Expand and write in standard form: \(4x^2 + 4x + 1 = 0\), so \(a = 4\), \(b = 4\), \(c = 1\)
  2. Discriminant: \(\Delta = 4^2 - 4(4)(1) = 16 - 16 = 0\)
  3. Apply the formula: \(x = \frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{0}}{2(4)} = \frac{-4}{8} = -\dfrac{1}{2}\)
  4. One repeated solution: \(x = -\dfrac{1}{2}\)

Worked example: Use the discriminant and quadratic formula for \(x^2 + 6x + 9 = 0\).

  1. Here \(a = 1\), \(b = 6\), \(c = 9\), so \(\Delta = 36 - 36 = 0\) (one real solution)
  2. \[x = \frac{-6 \pm \sqrt{0}}{2} = \frac{-6}{2} = -3\]

Worked example: For \(3x^2 - 2x + 5 = 0\), \(\Delta = (-2)^2 - 4(3)(5) = 4 - 60 = -56 < 0\), so the quadratic formula gives no real solutions (\(\sqrt{-56}\) is not a real number).

Check your understanding

Try Exercises 6 and 7 on the topic page.

docs.title: 7. Rearranging formulae
docs.text:
To make a variable the subject of a formula, treat the formula like an equation and undo operations in reverse order (inverse operations).

Strategy:

  1. Identify the structure: sums, products, powers, and parentheses
  2. Decide which variable to isolate
  3. Undo operations starting with the one furthest from that variable
  4. Apply the same operation to both sides at each step

Worked example: Make \(r\) the subject of \(V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3\).

  1. Divide both sides by \(\dfrac{4}{3}\pi\): \(r^3 = \dfrac{3V}{4\pi}\)
  2. Take the cube root: \(r = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3V}{4\pi}}\)

Worked example: Make \(r\) the subject of \(A = P(1 + r)^t\).

  1. Divide by \(P\): \(\dfrac{A}{P} = (1 + r)^t\)
  2. Take the \(t\)th root: \(1 + r = \left(\dfrac{A}{P}\right)^{1/t}\)
  3. Subtract 1: \(r = \left(\dfrac{A}{P}\right)^{1/t} - 1\)

Key idea: rearranging uses the same balance rules as solving equations — only the variable you want to isolate is treated as unknown; all other letters are constants for that step.

Check your understanding

Try Exercise 8 on the topic page.

docs.title: Differential Calculus
docs.text:
Differential Calculus

Work through these tutorials after watching the Differential Calculus videos. Each subsection walks through the main ideas step by step with worked examples. The exercises on the topic page use different problems — they are not copies of the tutorial or video examples.

docs.title: 1. Derivatives and tangents
docs.text:
A function can be increasing (as \(x\) increases, \(y\) increases) or decreasing (as \(x\) increases, \(y\) decreases). Often the rate of change itself changes along the graph.

The tangent line at a point on a graph is the straight line that touches the curve and matches its slope at that point. Because a tangent is a line \(y = ax + b\), its slope \(a\) gives a number for the instantaneous rate of change.

docs.title: From secant to derivative
docs.text:
Pick a point \((x_0, f(x_0))\) and a nearby point. The line through them is a secant. Its slope is

\[\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\]

As the second point moves closer ( \(\Delta x \to 0\) ), the secant approaches the tangent. The derivative is this limiting slope:

\[f'(x) = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\]

In practice we use differentiation rules rather than evaluating the limit each time.

docs.title: Notation
docs.text:
The derivative of \(f(x)\) can be written as:

  • \(f'(x)\)
  • \(\dfrac{df}{dx}\) or \(\dfrac{d}{dx}f(x)\)

docs.title: What the derivative tells you
docs.text:
Sign of \(f'(x)\) Meaning \(f'(x) > 0\) \(f\) is increasing at \(x\) \(f'(x) < 0\) \(f\) is decreasing at \(x\) \(f'(x) = 0\) horizontal tangent — possible local maximum or local minimum

Summary: \(f'(x)\) is the slope of the tangent at \(x\), and it measures the rate of change of \(f\) at that point.

Check your understanding

Try Exercises 7 and 8 on the topic page.

docs.title: 2. Differentiation rules
docs.text: This topic covers basic rules only. The product rule, quotient rule, and chain rule are not included here.

docs.title: Simple rules
docs.text:
Rule Formula Sum \((f + g)' = f' + g'\) Difference \((f - g)' = f' - g'\) Constant multiple \((c \cdot f)' = c \cdot f'\)

Differentiate term by term. Constants differentiate to \(0\).

docs.title: Common derivatives
docs.text:
Function \(f(x)\) Derivative \(f'(x)\) \(c\) (constant) \(0\) \(x^n\) \(nx^{n-1}\) \(e^x\) \(e^x\) \(\ln x\) \(\dfrac{1}{x}\) \(\sin x\) \(\cos x\) \(\cos x\) \(-\sin x\)

Power rule: \(\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^n) = nx^{n-1}\). This also works for negative exponents, e.g. \(\dfrac{1}{x^2} = x^{-2}\).

Worked example: Differentiate \(f(x) = \dfrac{1}{2}x^4 + 2x^3 - x^2 + x - 7\).

Differentiate each term:

\[f'(x) = 2x^3 + 6x^2 - 2x + 1\]

Worked example: Differentiate \(f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x^2} + \ln x - \cos x\).

Rewrite \(\dfrac{1}{x^2} = x^{-2}\):

\[f'(x) = -2x^{-3} + \dfrac{1}{x} + \sin x\]

Worked example — find a minimum: For \(f(x) = 3e^x - 6x\), find where \(f'(x) = 0\).

\[f'(x) = 3e^x - 6\]

Set \(f'(x) = 0\):

\[3e^x = 6 \;\Rightarrow\; e^x = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; x = \ln 2 \approx 0.693\]

At a minimum the tangent is horizontal, so \(f'(x) = 0\). To confirm it is a minimum (not a maximum), check that \(f'\) is negative to the left of \(x = \ln 2\) and positive to the right — or sketch the graph.

The minimum value is:

\[f(\ln 2) = 3e^{\ln 2} - 6\ln 2 = 6 - 6\ln 2 \approx 1.84\] Check your understanding

Try Exercises 1, 2, 9, and 10 on the topic page.

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
Functions

Work through these tutorials after watching the Functions videos. Each subsection walks through the main ideas step by step with worked examples. The exercises on the topic page use different problems — they are not copies of the tutorial or video examples.

docs.title: 1. Functions and graphs
docs.text:
A function takes an input and returns exactly one output. We often write the input as \(x\) and the output as \(y = f(x)\).

Term Meaning Domain All input values \(x\) for which the function is defined Range All possible output values \(f(x)\)

Key property: one input cannot give two different outputs. Different inputs may give the same output.

A function can be represented by a formula, a table, a graph, or a word description. In calculations we usually prefer a formula.

docs.title: Function notation
docs.text:
In \(f(x) = 2x + 3\):

  • \(f\) is the name of the function
  • \(x\) is the variable (input)
  • \(f(x)\) is the output value when the input is \(x\)

Worked example — evaluate: Find \(f(5)\) when \(f(x) = 2x + 3\).

Substitute \(x = 5\): \(f(5) = 2(5) + 3 = 13\).

Worked example — solve: If \(f(x) = 5\) and \(f(x) = 2x + 3\), find \(x\).

Here \(5\) is an output, not “\(x = 5\)”. Solve:

\[2x + 3 = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; 2x = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 1\]

Worked example — domain and range: For \(f(x) = \sqrt{2x}\), find the domain and range.

  • \(\sqrt{2x}\) requires \(2x \ge 0\), so domain is \([0, \infty)\).
  • Square roots are non-negative, so range is \([0, \infty)\).

Worked example — reading a graph: The graph of \(f(x) = \sqrt{2x}\) passes through \((8, 4)\).

That means \(f(8) = 4\).

To find the input when the output is \(5\), use the graph (or solve \(\sqrt{2x} = 5\)):

\[2x = 25 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 12.5\]

So when \(f(x) = 5\), the input is \(12.5\).

Check your understanding

Try Exercise 1 and Exercise 5 on the topic page.

docs.title: Linear functions
docs.text:
\[f(x) = ax + b\]
  • \(a\) is the slope (rate of change)
  • \(b\) is the \(y\)-intercept

Linear growth has a constant rate of change: increasing \(x\) by 1 always changes \(y\) by \(a\).

Worked example: From the graph of a line with slope \(2\) and \(y\)-intercept \(-1\), write the formula.

\[f(x) = 2x - 1\]

docs.title: Power functions
docs.text:
\[f(x) = b x^a \qquad (x > 0 \text{ when needed for non-integer } a)\]
  • \(b = f(1)\) (value at \(x = 1\))
  • The exponent \(a\) controls shape:
  • \(a < 0\): decreasing
  • \(0 < a < 1\): increasing, but flattening
  • \(a > 1\): increasing, getting steeper

For power functions, a percentage change in \(x\) leads to a predictable percentage change in \(y\) (multiply \(x\) by \(k\), multiply \(y\) by \(k^a\)).

Worked example: Three power functions all pass through \((1, 2)\), so \(b = 2\) for each. Examples include \(f(x) = 2x^{-1}\), \(f(x) = 2x^{0.5}\), and \(f(x) = 2x^2\) — same \(b\), different \(a\).

docs.title: Exponential functions
docs.text:
\[f(x) = b \cdot a^x \qquad (a > 0,\; a \ne 1)\]
  • \(b\) is the initial value (\(f(0) = b\))
  • \(a\) is the growth factor
  • \(0 < a < 1\): decreasing (each step multiplies by \(a\))
  • \(a > 1\): increasing

Worked example: \(f(x) = 2 \cdot 0.4^x\) has \(b = 2\) and \(a = 0.4\). Each time \(x\) increases by 1, the value is multiplied by \(0.4\) (a decrease of 60%).

Worked example: \(f(x) = 2 \cdot 1.6^x\) has \(b = 2\) and \(a = 1.6\). Each time \(x\) increases by 1, the value increases by 60%.

Exponential functions have a rate of change proportional to the current value — the graph gets steeper as \(y\) grows.

Check your understanding

Try Exercises 2 and 3 on the topic page.

docs.title: 3. Inverse functions
docs.text:
The inverse function \(f^{-1}\) reverses \(f\): if \(f(x) = y\), then \(f^{-1}(y) = x\).

An inverse exists only when \(f\) is one-to-one (each output comes from exactly one input). Use the horizontal line test: if some horizontal line hits the graph more than once, there is no inverse on that domain.

Example: \(f(x) = x^2\) is not one-to-one on all real numbers (both \(2\) and \(-2\) give output \(4\)). Restricting to \(x \ge 0\) allows \(\sqrt{x}\) as the inverse.

docs.title: Finding an inverse algebraically
docs.text:
1. Write \(y = f(x)\)
  2. Swap \(x\) and \(y\)
  3. Solve for \(y\)
  4. Write the result as \(f^{-1}(x)\)

Worked example: Find the inverse of \(g(x) = 3x + 2\).

  1. \(y = 3x + 2\)
  2. Swap: \(x = 3y + 2\)
  3. Solve: \(y = \dfrac{x - 2}{3}\)
  4. So \(g^{-1}(x) = \dfrac{x - 2}{3}\)

The graphs of \(f\) and \(f^{-1}\) are reflections of each other in the line \(y = x\).

docs.title: Logarithms as inverses of exponentials
docs.text:
If \(f(x) = 10^x\), then \(f^{-1}(x) = \log_{10}(x)\).

\(x\) \(10^x\) \(\log_{10}(x)\) 0 1 — 1 10 0 2 100 1 3 1000 2

So \(\log_{10}(1000) = 3\) because \(10^3 = 1000\).

Similarly, \(f(x) = e^x\) has inverse \(f^{-1}(x) = \ln(x)\) (natural logarithm), where \(e \approx 2.718\).

Useful facts:

  • \(e^0 = 1\), so \(\ln(1) = 0\)
  • \(\ln(x)\) is defined only for \(x > 0\) (you cannot take \(\ln(0)\))

Worked example: Simplify \(\log_{10}(10^x)\).

Since \(\log_{10}\) undoes \(10^x\), the result is \(x\) (for \(x > 0\) in the inner exponential when working with real logarithms in applied settings).

Check your understanding

Try Exercises 6, 8, and 10 on the topic page.

docs.title: Integral Calculus
docs.text:
Integral Calculus

Work through these tutorials after watching the Integral Calculus videos. Each subsection walks through the main ideas step by step with worked examples. The exercises on the topic page use different problems — they are not copies of the tutorial or video examples.

docs.title: 1. Indefinite integrals and antiderivatives
docs.text:
Differentiation finds the rate of change \(f(x) = F'(x)\). Integration reverses this process.

If \(F'(x) = f(x)\), then \(F\) is an antiderivative of \(f\). We often use a capital letter for antiderivatives.

Example: For \(f(x) = 2x\), one antiderivative is \(F(x) = x^2\), because \(\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^2) = 2x\).

Adding any constant also works: \(x^2 + 73\) is still an antiderivative of \(2x\), since the derivative of a constant is \(0\).

The indefinite integral collects all antiderivatives:

\[\int f(x)\, dx = F(x) + C\]

Always include \(dx\) (the variable of integration) and \(+C\) (the arbitrary constant).

docs.title: Rules for indefinite integrals
docs.text:
Rule Formula Sum \(\int (f + g)\, dx = \int f\, dx + \int g\, dx\) Difference \(\int (f - g)\, dx = \int f\, dx - \int g\, dx\) Constant multiple \(\int c \cdot f\, dx = c \int f\, dx\)

Integrate term by term. This topic does not cover substitution or partial fractions.

docs.title: Common antiderivatives
docs.text:
Function \(f(x)\) Antiderivative \(\int f(x)\, dx\) \(c\) (constant) \(cx + C\) \(x^n\) (\(n \ne -1\)) \(\dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C\) \(e^x\) \(e^x + C\) \(\dfrac{1}{x}\) \(\ln\lvert x\rvert + C\) \(\sin x\) \(-\cos x + C\) \(\cos x\) \(\sin x + C\)

Power rule for integration: add 1 to the exponent, then divide by the new exponent.

Worked example: Evaluate \(\int (x^3 + x^2)\, dx\).

\[\int x^3\, dx + \int x^2\, dx = \frac{x^4}{4} + \frac{x^3}{3} + C\]

Worked example: Evaluate \(\int \left(\dfrac{1}{x^2} + 3x - \sin x\right) dx\).

Rewrite \(\dfrac{1}{x^2} = x^{-2}\):

\[\int x^{-2}\, dx + 3\int x\, dx - \int \sin x\, dx = -\frac{1}{x} + \frac{3x^2}{2} + \cos x + C\]

Worked example: Evaluate \(\int (2x^3 - 7)\, dx\) and check by differentiating.

\[\int 2x^3\, dx - \int 7\, dx = \frac{1}{2}x^4 - 7x + C\]

Check: \(\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}x^4 - 7x + C\right) = 2x^3 - 7\) ✓

Check your understanding

Try Exercises 1, 2, 3, and 4 on the topic page.

docs.title: 2. Definite integrals and areas
docs.text:
A definite integral from \(a\) to \(b\) gives the signed area between the graph of \(f\) and the \(x\)-axis on \([a, b]\):

  • Area above the axis counts as positive
  • Area below the axis counts as negative

docs.title: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
docs.text:
If \(F'(x) = f(x)\), then

\[\int_a^b f(x)\, dx = F(b) - F(a)\]

Any antiderivative works — the constant \(C\) cancels in the difference. Usually choose \(C = 0\).

Notation: \(\displaystyle\left[F(x)\right]_a^b = F(b) - F(a)\)

Type Result Indefinite integral \(\int f(x)\, dx\) A family of functions (\(+ C\)) Definite integral \(\int_a^b f(x)\, dx\) A number (signed area)

Worked example — area under a line: Find \(\displaystyle\int_0^5 (x - 2)\, dx\).

An antiderivative is \(F(x) = \dfrac{x^2}{2} - 2x\):

\[\left[\frac{x^2}{2} - 2x\right]_0^5 = \left(\frac{25}{2} - 10\right) - 0 = 2.5\]

The graph crosses the \(x\)-axis at \(x = 2\); regions above and below the axis combine to give signed area \(2.5\).

Worked example — signed area: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_{-1}^1 (6x^2 - 4)\, dx\).

\[F(x) = 2x^3 - 4x\] \[F(1) - F(-1) = (2 - 4) - (-2 + 4) = -2 - 2 = -4\]

The negative result reflects more area below the axis than above on \([-1, 1]\).

Worked example: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi} \sin x\, dx\).

An antiderivative is \(-\cos x\):

\[\left[-\cos x\right]_0^{\pi} = (-\cos\pi) - (-\cos 0) = -(-1) - (-1) = 1 + 1 = 2\] Check your understanding

Try Exercises 5, 6, 7, 8, and 10 on the topic page.

docs.title: Vectors in 2D
docs.text:
Vectors in 2D

Work through these tutorials after watching the Vectors in 2D videos. Each subsection walks through the main ideas step by step with worked examples. The exercises on the topic page use different problems — they are not copies of the tutorial or video examples.

docs.title: 1. Vectors in 2D
docs.text:
A vector has both magnitude (length) and direction. A scalar has only a value (no direction).

Vector Scalar Has direction? Yes No Examples velocity, force, displacement speed, mass, temperature

Vectors are drawn as arrows. We write \(\vec{a}\) (arrow over the letter) to show it is a vector, or use bold \(\mathbf{a}\) in printed text. Sometimes a bar, \(\bar{a}\), is used instead, but the arrow \(\vec{a}\) is the standard notation for vectors. Here we use \(\mathbf{a}\) for vectors.

docs.title: Component form
docs.text:
A 2D vector is written as a column vector:

\[\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{pmatrix}\]

where \(a_1\) is the horizontal component (top entry) and \(a_2\) is the vertical component (bottom entry).

Worked example: Draw \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}\).

From the origin: one step left (\(-1\)), then three steps up (\(3\)). The dashed lines show the horizontal and vertical components. The same vector can be drawn anywhere — only magnitude and direction matter.

docs.title: Scaling
docs.text:
Multiply each component by the scalar \(c\):

\[c\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} ca_1 \\ ca_2 \end{pmatrix}\]
  • \(c > 1\) stretches the vector
  • \(0 < c < 1\) shortens it
  • \(c = -1\) reverses direction (\(-\mathbf{a}\))

Worked example: If \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}\), find \(3\mathbf{a}\) and \(-\mathbf{a}\).

\[3\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 9 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad -\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix}\]

docs.title: Addition and subtraction
docs.text:
Add or subtract component by component:

\[\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} a_1 + b_1 \\ a_2 + b_2 \end{pmatrix}\] \[\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} a_1 - b_1 \\ a_2 - b_2 \end{pmatrix}\]

Geometrically, place vectors tip-to-tail (or use the parallelogram rule). Note that \(\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{a} + (-\mathbf{b})\).

Worked example: With \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}\):

\[\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} -5 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\] Check your understanding

Try Exercise 1 and Exercise 7 on the topic page.

docs.title: 2. Dot product
docs.text:
The dot product (also called scalar product) of \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{pmatrix}\) is:

\[\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2\]

The result is a scalar, not a vector.

Worked example: For \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\):

\[\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = (-3)(1) + (4)(2) = -3 + 8 = 5\]

docs.title: Length of a vector
docs.text:
\[|\mathbf{a}|^2 = \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{a} = a_1^2 + a_2^2, \qquad |\mathbf{a}| = \sqrt{a_1^2 + a_2^2}\]

This is the Pythagorean theorem applied to the components.

Worked example: For \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\):

\[|\mathbf{a}| = \sqrt{(-3)^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5\]

docs.title: Angle between vectors
docs.text:
\[\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = |\mathbf{a}|\,|\mathbf{b}|\cos\theta\]

So (when both vectors are nonzero):

\[\cos\theta = \frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{|\mathbf{a}|\,|\mathbf{b}|}\]

Worked example — perpendicular vectors: For \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{1}{3} \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\):

\[\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = 6 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + (-2)(1) = 2 - 2 = 0\]

So \(\cos\theta = 0\) and \(\theta = 90°\). The vectors are orthogonal (perpendicular). We write \(\mathbf{a} \perp \mathbf{b}\).

docs.title: Projection
docs.text:
The projection of \(\mathbf{a}\) onto \(\mathbf{b}\) is the component of \(\mathbf{a}\) in the direction of \(\mathbf{b}\):

\[\text{proj}_{\mathbf{b}}(\mathbf{a}) = \frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{|\mathbf{b}|^2}\,\mathbf{b}\]

Worked example: For \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 8 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\):

\[\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = 16 + 16 = 32, \qquad |\mathbf{b}|^2 = 64 + 4 = 68\] \[\text{proj}_{\mathbf{b}}(\mathbf{a}) = \frac{32}{68}\,\mathbf{b} = \frac{8}{17}\,\mathbf{b}\] Check your understanding

Try Exercises 2, 3, 4, and 5 on the topic page.

docs.title: 3. Parametric equation of a line
docs.text:
A point \(P_0\) with position vector \(\mathbf{r}_0\) and a direction vector \(\mathbf{d}\) determine a line. The parameter \(t\) controls how far we move along \(\mathbf{d}\):

\[\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{r}_0 + t\mathbf{d}\]

As \(t\) ranges over all real numbers, \(\mathbf{r}\) traces every point on the line.

In components, with \(\mathbf{r}_0 = \begin{pmatrix} x_0 \\ y_0 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{d} = \begin{pmatrix} d_1 \\ d_2 \end{pmatrix}\):

\[x = x_0 + t d_1, \qquad y = y_0 + t d_2\]

The same line can be described in many ways (different starting points or direction vectors).

Worked example: A line through \(\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\) with direction \(\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\):

\[\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\]

When \(t = 10\):

\[\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 + 30 \\ 0 + 20 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 33 \\ 20 \end{pmatrix}\]

Worked example — parallel line: Through \(\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\) with the same direction \(\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\):

\[\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\]

Worked example — orthogonal line: A direction vector orthogonal to \(\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\) satisfies \(3d_1 + 2d_2 = 0\). One choice is \(\mathbf{d} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}\). A line through \(\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\):

\[\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}\] Check your understanding

Try Exercises 8, 9, and 10 on the topic page.

docs.title: Vectors in 2D
docs.text:
Vectors in 2D

Work through the videos below in order. When you have watched them, take the quiz and try the exercises. A quiz score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding.

docs.title: Learning objectives
docs.text:
After working through this topic, you should be able to:

  • Represent vectors in the coordinate plane and perform vector algebra
  • Compute the dot product of two vectors and interpret the result
  • Use parametric equations to describe a line in 2D (optional)

docs.title: 1. Vectors in 2D
docs.text: Distinguish vectors from scalars, write vectors in component form, and add, subtract, and scale them.

docs.title: 2. Dot product
docs.text: Compute the dot product, find vector length, and determine angles between vectors.

docs.title: 3. Parametric equation of a line (optional)
docs.text: Describe a line in the plane using a position vector, a direction vector, and a parameter.

docs.title: Quiz
docs.text:
When you have been through the videos, test your understanding with the quiz below. The quiz contains 10 questions. A score of at least 7 out of 10 suggests sufficient understanding. You can see your total score at the bottom of the page, as well as your score for each question. You can retake the quiz as many times as you like — results are not stored or assessed.

Question 1Which of the following is a vector quantity?SpeedDisplacementMassTemperatureCheck AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: A vector has both magnitude and direction. Displacement specifies how far and in which direction something moves; speed, mass, and temperature are scalars (magnitude only).

Question 2Which notation correctly represents a vector with horizontal component $3$ and vertical component $-2$?$3 - 2$$\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}$$(3, -2)$$3 \times (-2)$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: A 2D vector is written as a column vector: the horizontal component on top and the vertical component below.

Question 3If $\mathbf{u} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$, what is $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}$?$\begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$$\begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$\begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Add components: $\begin{pmatrix} 1 + 4 \\ 2 + (-1) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

Question 4If $\mathbf{u} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$, what is $\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{v}$?$\begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}$$\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$$\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}$$\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Subtract components: $\begin{pmatrix} 5 - 2 \\ 3 - 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$.

Question 5What is $3\mathbf{a}$ when $\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix}$?$\begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$$\begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ -12 \end{pmatrix}$$\begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix}$$\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ -12 \end{pmatrix}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Multiply each component by the scalar: $3\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ -12 \end{pmatrix}$.

Question 6What is the dot product $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}$ for $\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$?811145Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = a_x b_x + a_y b_y = 2 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 1 = 8 + 3 = 11$.

Question 7What is the length $|\mathbf{a}|$ of $\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}$?$7$$5$$25$$\sqrt{7}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: $|\mathbf{a}| = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$.

Question 8If $\theta$ is the angle between nonzero vectors $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$, which expression equals $\cos\theta$?$\dfrac{|\mathbf{a}| |\mathbf{b}|}{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}$$\dfrac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{|\mathbf{a}| |\mathbf{b}|}$$\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}$$\dfrac{|\mathbf{a}| + |\mathbf{b}|}{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: The dot product formula is $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = |\mathbf{a}| |\mathbf{b}| \cos\theta$, so $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{|\mathbf{a}| |\mathbf{b}|}$.

Question 9Vectors $\mathbf{u} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}$ are perpendicular. Why?Because $|\mathbf{u}| = |\mathbf{v}|$Because $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$Because their components are equalBecause $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: Two nonzero vectors are perpendicular when their dot product is zero: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = 1 \cdot 4 + 2 \cdot (-2) = 4 - 4 = 0$.

Question 10A line is given by $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{r}_0 + t\mathbf{d}$ with $\mathbf{r}_0 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{d} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$. What is the position vector when $t = 2$?$\begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$$\begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}$$\begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$$\begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$Check AnswerScore: 0 / 1

Explanation: $\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} + 2\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 + 6 \\ 2 + 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}$.

docs.title: Exercise 1
docs.text:
Given \(\mathbf{u} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix}\), find:

  1. \(\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}\)
  2. \(\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{v}\)
  3. \(3\mathbf{u}\)
  1. \(\begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\)
  2. \(\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -6 \end{pmatrix}\)
  3. \(\begin{pmatrix} 12 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix}\)

docs.title: Exercise 2
docs.text:
Given \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}\), compute \(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}\).

\(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = 2 \cdot 3 + 4 \cdot (-2) = 6 - 8 = -2\)

docs.title: Exercise 3
docs.text:
Find the length \(|\mathbf{c}|\) when \(\mathbf{c} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix}\).

\(|\mathbf{c}| = \sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{36 + 64} = \sqrt{100} = 10\)

docs.title: Exercise 4
docs.text:
Find \(\cos\theta\) for the angle \(\theta\) between \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\).

\(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = 0\), \(|\mathbf{a}| = 3\), and \(|\mathbf{b}| = 4\), so \(\cos\theta = 0\). The vectors are perpendicular.

docs.title: Exercise 5
docs.text:
Are \(\mathbf{u} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 8 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}\) perpendicular? Justify your answer.

Yes. \(\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = 1 \cdot 8 + 4 \cdot (-2) = 8 - 8 = 0\), so the vectors are perpendicular.

docs.title: Exercise 6
docs.text:
Find the value of \(k\) such that \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ k \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\) satisfy \(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = 10\).

\(2 \cdot 3 + k \cdot 1 = 10 \;\Rightarrow\; 6 + k = 10 \;\Rightarrow\; k = 4\)

docs.title: Exercise 7
docs.text:
Given \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\), find \(2\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b}\).

\(2\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\)

docs.title: Exercise 8
docs.text:
A line passes through the origin with direction vector \(\mathbf{d} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\). Using \(\mathbf{r} = t\mathbf{d}\), find the point on the line when \(t = 3\).

\(\mathbf{r} = 3\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 12 \end{pmatrix}\)

docs.title: Exercise 9 (optional)
docs.text:
A line is given by \(\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}\). Find the position vector when \(t = 2\).

\(\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + 2\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 + 4 \\ 1 + 6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix}\)

docs.title: Exercise 10
docs.text:
Given \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\), find \(|\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b}|\).

\(\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}\), so \(|\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b}| = \sqrt{5^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{25 + 9} = \sqrt{34}\)
